result.question: How can a $55 SO-101 arm (LeRobot, ACT, 2x 640x480 RGB cams, no force sensing) pick and sort recyclable water bottles and caps by plastic type (PET vs HDPE vs PP) for easier downstream processing in Vietnam/HCMC — covering feasible plastic-ID techniques on cheap hardware, what transfers from commercial MRF robots, ACT vs decoupled perception training approaches, bottle/cap grasp physics within 0.2-0.3kg payload, and Vietnam ve-chai economics of which separations add value?
result.summary: Split the question along its five stated sub-domains, mapping each to a distinct evidence community: sensing/ID research (CV + cheap spectroscopy), commercial industry practice (MRF robots), robot-learning methodology (ACT/LeRobot vs classifier+policy), low-cost manipulation practice (SO-101 grasping deformable bottles), and local market economics (Vietnam informal recycling prices) — so no two queries surface the same source pool and the skeptical/economic angle can adversarially bound the technical ones.

angles.label: plastic-ID sensing on cheap hardware (academic/technical)
angles.query: plastic type classification PET HDPE PP RGB camera deep learning low-cost NIR spectroscopy limitations black plastic recycling
angles.rationale: Surfaces the core feasibility literature: what RGB-only vision can and cannot distinguish between resin types, resin-code detection papers, and the well-documented NIR failure modes (carbon-black, clear film) plus cheap sensor alternatives (AS7263-class NIR, density float-sink, capacitive).

angles.label: commercial robotic sorters — what transfers (industry practice)
angles.query: AMP Robotics ZenRobotics TOMRA how robotic waste sorting works sensors AI vision suction gripper materials recovery facility
angles.rationale: Establishes ground truth on how production systems actually identify and pick plastics (hyperspectral + suction at high speed), which reveals both the transferable ideas (vision-first classification, SKU/brand recognition) and the parts a $55 arm cannot replicate.

angles.label: training approach — ACT imitation vs decoupled perception (practitioner/implementation)
angles.query: SO-101 SO-ARM100 LeRobot ACT policy pick and place sorting task vs YOLO object detection classifier grasp pipeline fine-tuning custom dataset
angles.rationale: Targets hobbyist and LeRobot-community experience reports on whether end-to-end ACT handles multi-class sorting or whether a separate detector/classifier choosing the bin plus a simpler grasp policy is more reliable, including dataset sizes and fine-tuning workflows.

angles.label: grasping bottles and caps with low-payload arms (manipulation physics)
angles.query: robot gripper grasping empty crushed plastic bottles deformable thin-walled objects small caps low payload two-finger gripper strategies
angles.rationale: Covers the mechanical sub-problem the other angles ignore: deformable/crushed bottles, slippery labels, and 3cm caps within 0.2-0.3kg payload and 35-40cm reach — where suction is off the table and gripper geometry and pre-manipulation (nudging, edge-picking) dominate success rates.

angles.label: Vietnam ve chai economics — which separations pay (contrarian/skeptical)
angles.query: Vietnam ve chai scrap prices PET bottle HDPE PP cap price per kg VND informal recycling HCMC plastic value chain
angles.rationale: Adversarial economic check: verifies the claimed 20-35k VND/kg cap vs 4-7k PET spread, whether ve chai collectors already do this separation manually faster than any hobby arm could, and what throughput an arm would need for the sort to add any real value — bounding the build plan's realistic scope.

results.title: Deep Learning Meets Trash: AMP Robotics' Revolution in Materials Recovery (Robohub)
results.snippet: In-depth technical interview on how AMP actually works: RGB-camera-only deep learning (no hyperspectral needed) that classifies plastics by color, shape, opacity and brand/SKU cues, plus custom suction grippers. This is the strongest evidence that vision-first classification on ordinary cameras — the part a cheap SO-101 setup can replicate — is production-proven, while the delta-robot speed and vacuum system are what don't transfer.

results.title: AMP Robotics applies AI, robots to mounting recycling challenge (The Robot Report)
results.snippet: Details AMP's design choices: suction cup with compliant spring to handle variable item height and crushed/deformed material, cloud-trained neural network on off-the-shelf robots. Directly informs grasp-physics tradeoffs (suction vs finger gripper for crushed bottles) and the decoupled perception-then-pick architecture relevant to an ACT-vs-classifier decision.

results.title: NIR Spectroscopy for Plastic Sorting and Polymer Identification: basics and limitations
results.snippet: Explains how NIR spectral fingerprints distinguish PET/HDPE/PP and why the technique fails on black/dark plastics and thin films — establishing the ground truth on what cheap RGB vision cannot see (polymer chemistry) and why commercial MRFs pay for spectroscopy. Key input for deciding whether a hobby build should rely on shape/brand priors instead of polymer sensing.

results.title: TOMRA AUTOSORT BLACK: Advanced Sorting for Black Plastics
results.snippet: TOMRA's own documentation of the NIR blind spot: black plastics absorb all incident NIR light and need MIR-based FLYING BEAM hardware to sort — confirming that dark caps/bottles are a fundamental sensing limit, not a software problem, so a hobby system must fall back on color/shape/brand heuristics or density tests for dark items.

results.title: AMP Robotics: AI Platform for Small Format Recycling (US Plastics Pact case study)
results.snippet: Case study on AI sorting of small-format items — the category bottle caps fall into — showing AMP's Neuron platform differentiates objects by color, size, shape, opacity, and brand. Directly relevant to whether cap-level (HDPE/PP) sorting, the highest-value separation in the Vietnam ve chai context, is feasible with vision alone.

results.title: AMP Robotics Achieves Data Milestones and Recycling Automation Breakthrough
results.snippet: Primary-source numbers for the industrial baseline: 100+ recyclable categories classified, 120-140 picks/minute, billions of training images from real waste streams. Useful for calibrating the gap a 640x480 dual-cam SO-101 must accept (single-digit picks/minute, dozens-of-classes dataset) and for the dataset-scale argument favoring fine-tuned YOLO over end-to-end ACT for classification.

results.title: Design and Experimental Validation of a 3D-Printed Two-Finger Gripper with a V-Shaped Profile for Lightweight Waste Collection
results.snippet: Closest published analog to the exact sub-problem: a 3D-printed two-finger gripper with a 135-degree V-shaped fingertip profile achieving ~80% grasp success on typical lightweight waste items (bottles, disposable cups, plastic bags). Directly transferable gripper-geometry guidance for redesigning SO-101 fingertips for deformable bottles and caps.

results.title: Teaching a Robot Arm to Pick a Bottle: First Results with Pi0.5 Imitation Learning (SO-101 Pro)
results.snippet: Exact hardware + exact object class: an SO-101 Pro (STS3215 servos) trained via imitation learning to grasp and lift a bottle from ~100 teleop episodes. Gives a realistic baseline for demo counts, failure modes, and what bottle-grasping success looks like on this specific low-payload arm.

results.title: How I Trained Action Chunking Transformer (ACT) on SO-101: Journey, Gotchas, and Lessons
results.snippet: Practitioner writeup of ACT on the SO-101 covering camera setup, demo collection gotchas, and grasp reliability lessons — the practical bridge between the manipulation-physics constraints (payload, gripper travel) and the ACT training pipeline the build plan will use.

results.title: Learning Pregrasp Manipulation of Objects from Ungraspable Poses
results.snippet: Core reference for the pre-manipulation strategy the angle highlights: RL-learned pregrasp sliding pushes thin/flat objects (like a 3cm cap lying flat, or a flattened bottle) to a table edge or graspable pose before a parallel-jaw pick — a workaround for objects a two-finger gripper cannot pick directly.

results.title: Hybrid Gripper with Passive Pneumatic Soft Joints for Grasping Deformable Thin Objects
results.snippet: Addresses the crushed/thin-walled bottle problem head-on: walls of 0.3-0.5mm mean the gap between slip-and-drop and irreversible crushing is tiny without force sensing; passive compliance (soft joints/pads) substitutes for the force feedback the SO-101 lacks. Design principle transfers even if pneumatics don't.

results.title: In the Wild Ungraspable Object Picking with Bimanual Nonprehensile Manipulation
results.snippet: Demonstrates nonprehensile nudging/edge-picking to convert ungraspable poses into pickable ones in unstructured scenes — relevant to caps and flattened bottles on a flat sorting surface, though the bimanual setup exceeds a single SO-101 and would need adaptation (e.g., wall/fixture as second contact).

results.title: Bảng giá thu mua phế liệu nhựa (current VN scrap-plastic price table)
results.snippet: Live Vietnamese dealer price table by resin type — directly tests the claimed spread: quotes PP scrap at roughly 15,500-39,700 VND/kg and ABS 25,500-65,500 VND/kg, versus PET bottle scrap far lower. Confirms the direction of the cap (PP/HDPE) vs PET premium, but also shows huge dealer-to-dealer variance and volume-dependent pricing, meaning a hobby arm's few kg/day would land at the bottom of the range.

results.title: Bảng Giá Phế Liệu Nhựa cập nhật mới nhất (updated VN plastic scrap prices)
results.snippet: Second independent HCMC-area price sheet for cross-checking the 20-35k VND/kg cap vs 4-7k PET claim; lists PP purchase prices around 20,000-35,000 VND/kg and stresses prices depend on cleanliness, volume, and location — i.e., separation only pays if lots are large and clean enough to hit the high end, a key bound on what a single sorting arm can add.

results.title: IFC/World Bank: Market Study for Vietnam — Plastics Circularity Opportunities and Barriers
results.snippet: The authoritative value-chain study for PET/HDPE/PP in Vietnam: quantifies prices at each stage (picker → aggregator → recycler), the $2.2-2.9B/yr material value lost, and shows the informal sector already performs resin separation cheaply — the adversarial baseline any robot sort must beat. Includes stage-by-stage margin data needed to compute whether cap separation adds value at hobby-arm throughput.

results.title: ADB: Market Research — PET Recycling in Viet Nam
results.snippet: PET-specific economics: collection prices, aggregation margins, and why baled PET (with caps/labels) trades at low VND/kg — explains what downstream recyclers actually pay for cap-off vs cap-on bottles, i.e., the real per-kg delta a sorting arm could capture rather than the headline resin-price spread.

results.title: Viet Nam News: Self-employed scrap collectors vital for waste sorting
results.snippet: Documents that ve chai collectors already hand-sort bottles, caps, and resin types as a core part of the trade at ~100,000 VND (~$4) per day of labor — a human sorts hundreds of items/hour, so an arm doing seconds-per-pick must be valued against roughly 12,500 VND/hour displaced labor, the strongest contrarian check on the build's economics.

results.title: Waste Pickers in the Recycled Plastics Supply Chain (peer-reviewed, PMC)
results.snippet: Peer-reviewed analysis of where value accrues in the picker-to-recycler plastics chain (Vietnam among cases): pickers capture little of the resin-price spread because aggregators set prices by lot size and purity — implying a hobby arm's marginal separation gain flows mostly to the depot, not the operator, unless it sells directly at aggregator tier volumes.

results.title: Low-Cost Recognition of Plastic Waste Using Deep Learning and a Multi-Spectral Near-Infrared Sensor (Sensors, MDPI)
results.snippet: The single most on-point paper for this build: combines deep learning with a cheap multi-spectral NIR sensor (AS726x/AS7265x-class, tens of dollars, not a $20k spectrometer) to recognize plastic waste types. Directly answers whether a hobby-budget NIR add-on to the SO-101 can discriminate PET vs HDPE vs PP, including reported accuracy and the sensor's practical constraints (standoff distance, integration time, illumination) — exactly the 'cheap sensor add-on' feasibility question.

results.title: AI-based plastic waste classification for sorting purposes: A review on recent progresses and challenges (Waste Management, 2026)
results.snippet: Recent comprehensive review comparing RGB-camera deep learning against spectral methods for sorting: cites RGB CNN systems classifying PET/HDPE/PS/PP on micro-computers (~74% for 4 classes on microcontroller-grade hardware, 94-95% with YOLOv5/SVM in better setups), and frames the accuracy ceiling of RGB-only vision vs NIR. Ideal for calibrating what the SO-101's two 640x480 RGB cams can realistically achieve and where they will fail.

results.title: Application of Computer Vision Techniques for Segregation of Plastic Waste based on Resin Identification Code (arXiv:2011.07747)
results.snippet: Tests the exact hybrid-CV+heuristic idea in the research question: reading the molded resin identification code (the 1-7 triangle) with classical CV and learning methods instead of inferring polymer from appearance. Documents when code-reading works and its brutal failure modes (code not visible in pose, worn/molded low-contrast marks, low resolution) — critical for deciding whether the arm should re-orient bottles to find the code or rely on shape/color priors.

results.title: Deep learning-based construction and demolition plastic waste classification by resin type using RGB images (Resources, Conservation and Recycling)
results.snippet: Demonstrates resin-type classification from plain RGB images using transfer learning (ResNeXt-101, Swin Transformer) and, importantly for a Raspberry-Pi-class robot, lightweight MobileNetV3 models via knowledge distillation. Strong evidence for the 'decoupled perception' architecture: a small RGB classifier picks the bin while the ACT policy only handles grasping — with honest numbers on how far appearance alone gets you on resin type.

results.title: Spectral preprocessing across featureless NIR and feature-rich MIR: Machine learning classification of black plastics (Measurement)
results.snippet: Documents the canonical NIR failure mode the research question asks about: carbon-black plastics have near-zero NIR reflectance, making them 'featureless' to NIR sorters, and shows what preprocessing/ML can and cannot recover vs falling back to MIR/FTIR. Relevant for scoping which cap/bottle colors a cheap NIR add-on will simply never identify (dark HDPE/PP caps are common in the VN waste stream).

results.title: Vision-Based Plastic Identification: A Comprehensive Survey on the Deep Learning Methods (Springer)
results.snippet: Survey dedicated specifically to vision-only (camera-based) plastic identification with deep learning — catalogs datasets, YOLO/segmentation architectures, and reported accuracies across bottle/waste benchmarks. Useful as the map of available public datasets and fine-tuning baselines before collecting custom SO-101 bottle/cap data, though less specific about hardware cost than the other results.

results.title: How I Trained Action Chunking Transformer (ACT) on SO-101: My Journey, Gotchas, and Lessons
results.snippet: First-person practitioner report of the full ACT workflow on the exact target hardware (SO-101 + LeRobot): teleoperation data collection, episode counts, camera setup pitfalls, training gotchas, and what actually made the policy succeed or fail. Directly answers the 'what does end-to-end ACT realistically handle on this arm' half of the research angle.

results.title: Train an ACT Policy for an SO-101 Robot using LeRobot (Trelis Research)
results.snippet: Step-by-step implementation guide for training ACT on SO-101 for a pick-and-place task: dataset recording via teleop, recommended demonstration counts (~50+ per task with varied object positions), training config, and deployment/evaluation — the canonical fine-tuning workflow the build plan would follow for the imitation-learning route.

results.title: Poor ACT Policy Performance After Training (Robot Not Moving or Grasping Properly) - huggingface/lerobot Issue #2213
results.snippet: Community failure report where a 100k-step ACT policy on a custom SO-101 setup produced almost no meaningful motion despite clean training logs — concrete evidence of end-to-end ACT reliability pitfalls (dataset quality, camera views, normalization) that motivates the decoupled classifier+grasp fallback for multi-class sorting.

results.title: Benchmarking Vision-Language-Action Models on SO-101: Failure and Recovery Analysis
results.snippet: Systematic benchmark of learned policies on the SO-101 platform with failure-mode and recovery analysis on tabletop manipulation (including sorting-style tasks) — quantifies how end-to-end policies actually fail on this exact $100-class arm, useful for adversarially scoping success rates in the build plan.

results.title: Robotic Waste Sorter with Agile Manipulation and Quickly Trainable Detector
results.snippet: Research implementation of the decoupled-perception architecture applied specifically to waste sorting: a quickly fine-tunable object detector chooses the item/bin while a separate manipulation module executes the pick — the strongest published template for the 'classifier picks bin, simpler policy grasps' alternative to ACT for bottle/cap sorting.

results.title: Imitation Learning for Robots: ACT, Diffusion Policy, VLAs — Practical Guide (2026) | SVRC
results.snippet: Practitioner-oriented 2026 guide comparing ACT, Diffusion Policy, and VLAs for hobby-class arms, including when imitation learning is the right tool versus detection-based pipelines, typical dataset sizes, and reliability expectations — useful framing for the ACT-vs-decoupled decision, though not SO-101-sorting-specific.

claims.claim: AMP Robotics identifies recyclables using camera imaging of the conveyor belt plus deep learning perception (no NIR/hyperspectral sensing is described), and operators can reconfigure pick targets in software with a few clicks — implying vision-only classification of plastic categories is industrially viable.
claims.quote: deep learning, with enough data, can tackle this complexity ... they can configure what the robot will pick with just a few clicks

claims.claim: AMP's vision-sorted material categories include PET bottles, natural and colored HDPE (milk jugs), and polypropylene — i.e., the same resin-type separations (PET vs HDPE vs PP) targeted in the SO-101 project are distinguished by camera + deep learning in production MRFs.
claims.quote: Primary targets: aluminum cans, cardboard, PET bottles, HDPE milk jugs, colored HDPE, polypropylene.

claims.claim: AMP's end effector is a suction cup gripper with a passive spring for mechanical compliance to absorb variable material height — a design choice (suction over fingered grasping) directly relevant to bottle/cap grasp strategy on a low-payload hobby arm.
claims.quote: a suction cup gripper here and a beefy spring

claims.claim: AMP robots achieve 80+ picks per minute (sometimes over 100) versus 30-50 for human sorters, with bale purity targets of about 85% for plastics and 97% for aluminum — benchmarks a hobby SO-101 arm cannot approach on throughput but can target on purity.
claims.quote: 30 to 50 picks per minute ... 80 plus picks per minute ... 85% pure ... 97% pure

claims.claim: AMP's models require ongoing retraining because waste stream composition drifts over time, implying a custom bottle/cap classifier for the Vietnam waste stream would also need continuous data collection and model maintenance rather than one-time training.
claims.quote: our models need to evolve as the industry and materials change. Model maintenance is crucial.

claims.claim: NIR spectroscopy identifies plastic types because each polymer absorbs specific near-infrared wavelengths (700–2500 nm) corresponding to its molecular structure, producing a distinct spectral fingerprint that distinguishes PET, HDPE, PP, PS, etc.
claims.quote: When NIR radiation illuminates a plastic surface [the polymer] absorbs specific wavelengths corresponding to its molecular structure... a distinct spectral fingerprint based on its chemical composition.

claims.claim: Black plastics containing carbon black pigments strongly absorb NIR radiation across a broad spectral range, making them effectively undetectable ('spectrally invisible') to NIR sorters — a hard limit for NIR-based sorting of dark bottles/caps.
claims.quote: Many black plastics contain carbon black pigments, which strongly absorb NIR radiation across a broad spectral range... [causing them to appear] spectrally invisible.

claims.claim: Surface contamination — labels, adhesives, and dirt — alters the measured NIR spectrum and can degrade identification accuracy, which matters for real-world bottles with labels on.
claims.quote: [Labels, adhesives, and dirt] can influence the measured spectrum.

claims.claim: Multilayer packaging produces ambiguous NIR spectral signatures, preventing determination of the dominant polymer type.
claims.quote: [Multilayer packaging produces] ambiguous spectral signatures [and] inability to determine dominant polymer type.

claims.claim: Portable/handheld NIR spectrometers are used in practice for quality control and manual verification of plastic streams (relevant as a possible point-check add-on rather than inline sensing), though the article gives no pricing.
claims.quote: portable NIR spectrometers [used for] quality control [and] manual verification.

claims.claim: AMP Robotics' AI sorting platform classifies recyclables using purely visual features captured by cameras — color, size, shape, opacity, and brand — rather than requiring NIR spectroscopy, implying RGB-vision-based plastic-type identification is viable at commercial scale and could transfer in principle to a cheap camera-only hobby arm.
claims.quote: The AI system can differentiate objects by "color, size, shape, opacity, brand, and more."

claims.claim: AMP's machine-learning system can classify more than 100 different categories and characteristics of recyclables across single-stream recycling, e-scrap, and construction and demolition debris, far exceeding the handful of classes (PET/HDPE/PP bottles and caps) a hobby sorter would need.
claims.quote: classify more than 100 different categories and characteristics of recyclables across single-stream recycling, e-scrap, and construction and demolition debris

claims.claim: AMP's robotic sorters pick at upwards of 80 items per minute, more than twice the speed of human sorters — a throughput benchmark against which an SO-101 arm (realistically a few picks per minute) is orders of magnitude slower, bounding the economic comparison to manual ve chai sorting.
claims.quote: upwards of 80 items per minute—more than two times as fast as human sorters

claims.claim: AMP demonstrated vision-based recovery of small-format polypropylene items (#5 plastic, e.g., K-Cup pods, yogurt containers, butter tubs) in partnership with Keurig Dr Pepper, showing that small PP objects — the same resin class as most bottle caps — can be visually identified and robotically sorted.
claims.quote: yogurt containers, butter tubs, and a whole host of other everyday products, including K-Cup pods

claims.claim: AMP's technology is deployed at commercial scale — roughly 200 deployments in more than 25 U.S. states processing more than 10 billion items annually — indicating the vision-based approach is production-proven, not experimental.
claims.quote: "more than 10 billion items annually" processed across approximately 200 deployments

claims.claim: Black polymers are traditionally undetectable by standard optical sorting sensors (NIR-based), and TOMRA requires optional add-on technologies beyond its base AUTOSORT sensor configuration to identify them — confirming that dark/black plastics defeat conventional NIR sorting.
claims.quote: Optional add-on technologies expand material identification capabilities for traditionally undetectable fractions like black polymers

claims.claim: TOMRA's commercial sorting machines rely on deep learning (artificial neural networks) as a core identification technology, not just spectroscopy — supporting the transferability of camera+neural-network classification to hobby-scale sorters.
claims.quote: TOMRA's deep learning technology is built on artificial neural networks

claims.claim: TOMRA's AUTOSORT platform uses a multi-sensor fusion approach combining several proprietary sensing technologies (SHARP EYE, GAINnext, DEEP LAISER, FLYING BEAM) rather than a single sensor modality to sort plastics.
claims.quote: Multifunctional sensor configuration

claims.claim: The dedicated AUTOSORT BLACK product page no longer exists as a standalone page; the URL now serves TOMRA's general AUTOSORT overview, which provides no throughput, purity, or polymer-specific (PET/HDPE/PP) performance specifications.
claims.quote: Page Title: AUTOSORT: Automated Sorting Systems for Recycling | TOMRA ... No specific throughput rates (tonnes/hour), percentages, or millimeter sizes are provided on this page.

result.publishDate: unknown (page footer shows © 2026 TOMRA Systems ASA; no article date)

claims.claim: AMP Robotics' commercial sorting AI (Neuron platform) identifies recyclables using visual features from cameras — colors, textures, shapes, sizes, patterns, and brand labels — rather than NIR spectroscopy, meaning RGB-camera-based classification is the core sensing modality used by a leading commercial robotic sorter and is in principle the same modality available on a cheap 640x480-camera hobby arm.
claims.quote: The AMP Neuron™ AI platform continuously trains itself by recognizing different colors, textures, shapes, sizes, patterns, and even brand labels.

claims.claim: As of mid-2021, AMP's vision system could classify more than 100 different categories and characteristics of recyclables across single-stream recycling, e-scrap, and construction/demolition streams.
claims.quote: The company can now classify more than 100 different categories and characteristics of recyclables

claims.claim: AMP's industrial sorting robots achieve pick speeds of up to 140 picks per minute on a single robot, with sustained rates of 120 picks per minute — roughly two picks per second, orders of magnitude beyond what an SO-101 hobby arm running ACT can achieve, so commercial throughput economics do not transfer to a $55 arm.
claims.quote: recording speeds of up to 140 picks per minute on a single robot and a sustained rate of 120 picks per minute

claims.claim: AMP's classification accuracy rests on data scale: its deployed fleet recognizes more than 10 billion items annually, forming what the company calls the largest known real-world dataset of recyclables for machine learning — a data advantage a hobby project cannot replicate, implying a custom fine-tuned classifier on a narrow bottle/cap domain is the realistic transfer path.
claims.quote: AMP has extended its object recognition run rate to more than 10 billion items annually.

claims.claim: Faster picking translated to a recovery increase of more than 50% on unevenly distributed material streams, indicating that in commercial MRF settings the binding constraint is pick throughput on messy streams, not classification.
claims.quote: This can translate to an increase of more than 50% in recovery for unevenly distributed material streams.

claims.claim: Training ACT on SO-101 with only 50 demonstrations collected from 5 fixed object positions produced a complete task failure: the policy learned to repeatedly peck at the table instead of grasping the block. This sets a lower bound on demo diversity needed for a bottle/cap pick task.
claims.quote: My SO-101 didn't learn to pick up the block, but rather, to _peck_ at the table over and over again…

claims.claim: With 50 training episodes covering 12 start poses per bin across 6 bins, the ACT policy achieved 60% in-distribution success but only 10% out-of-distribution success, showing ACT on SO-101 generalizes poorly beyond the demonstrated object placements.
claims.quote: in-distribution eval: 60% success rate (passing grade!). Average progress score 0.68. out-of-distribution eval: 10% success rate (ouch).

claims.claim: Increasing coverage to 25 episodes per bin (~150 episodes) with added yaw rotation variations (-45 to +45 degrees) and stratified sampling raised performance to 90% in-distribution and 75% out-of-distribution success — evidence that a few hundred well-stratified demos can make an SO-101 ACT pick-and-place policy usable, relevant to scoping the bottle/cap sorting data-collection budget.
claims.quote: In distribution: 90% success rate. Average progress score 0.92. Out of distribution: 75% success rate!

claims.claim: The author's working setup used two webcams, moved to a front-and-top placement to reduce occlusion during grasping — directly matching the 2x 640x480 camera configuration in the research question and suggesting front+top is the preferred layout.
claims.quote: Two webcams

claims.claim: Identical USB webcams caused recorder crashes roughly every 5 episodes during data collection, and the fix was udev rules mapping USB ports to invariant properties — a concrete data-collection gotcha that would affect any SO-101 bottle-sorting training pipeline using two identical cheap cameras.
claims.quote: My cameras would disconnect every ~5 episodes and crash the recorder script... Camera & arm udev rules: This is the solution to the frequent camera disconnection!

claims.claim: A 135° V-shaped fingertip profile on a 3D-printed two-finger gripper achieved a 100% grasp success rate on rigid 66 mm PET bottles (24–71 mm diameter range), whereas a conventional 45° V-profile produced only unstable flat-surface contact and failed to securely grasp bottles of that size.
claims.quote: Physical tests confirmed reliable operation across objects ranging from 24 to 71 mm in diameter, with a 100% success rate on rigid 66 mm bottles and a 40% success rate on deformable items

claims.claim: A single hobby-grade servomotor (Hitec HS-945MG, 1.079 N·m at 6.0 V) driving a four-bar parallel linkage in PLA is sufficient to grasp and retain waste objects up to 0.6 kg with an FEA-validated safety factor of 3.5, at an in-house material cost of USD 20–40 and about 10 hours of desktop 3D-printer time.
claims.quote: The proposed design offers significant advantages in terms of rapid prototyping (a print time of approximately 10 h), modularity, and low manufacturing cost (with an estimated in-house material cost of USD 20 to 40).

claims.claim: Deformable waste items (plastic bags, crushed/soft objects) are substantially harder to grasp than rigid ones with a rigid parallel-jaw gripper: success dropped from 100% on rigid objects to 40–60% on deformables, implying crushed bottles will be a distinct failure mode versus intact ones.
claims.quote: The gripper achieved a 100% success rate for rigid objects and 60% for deformable items, such as plastic bags.

claims.claim: Widening the fingertip V-angle from 45° to 135° increased grasp contact area from 152 mm² to 380 mm², prevents rotational slip of cylindrical items like bottles and cans, and produces a self-centering effect that reduces sensitivity to positioning errors — useful for vision-only arms with no force sensing.
claims.quote: Compared to a 45° configuration, the 135° profile increased the contact area from 152 mm2 to 380 mm2, allowing secure grasping of larger cylindrical objects within the specified fingertip height.

claims.claim: In lever-based parallel grippers, transmitted grip force is inversely related to jaw opening (max 35.97 N at the 24 mm minimum opening), and the authors identify the absence of sensory feedback as the key limitation for adaptability in cluttered environments — both directly relevant to sizing an SO-101 gripper for caps (~30 mm) vs bottles (~66 mm).
claims.quote: First, the absence of sensory feedback currently limits adaptability in dynamic or cluttered environments.

claims.claim: Objects too flat to grasp directly (e.g., large flat boxes, analogous to crushed bottles) can be made graspable without force sensing by pushing them against a fixed support surface and pivot-lifting one side, using a model-free DRL policy that relies only on RGB vision and proprioception.
claims.quote: The robot arm learns to first push the object towards a support surface and establishes a pivot to lift up one side of the object, thus creating a clearance between the object and the table for possible grasping solutions.

claims.claim: The method deliberately uses only a cheap RGB camera for external sensing (no force-torque sensor), in contrast to prior pregrasp work (Hou et al.) that required an FT sensor and precise table-shape knowledge.
claims.quote: Our work strikes a good balance between cheap and expressive external sensing (RGB camera) and generality of the target object (any rigid object).

claims.claim: Training compute is hobbyist-feasible: the SAC pregrasp policy converges in about one hour on a desktop CPU without a GPU, and transfers directly sim-to-real via domain randomization of mass, friction, and positions.
claims.quote: The policy is trained on i7-8700K without GPU, and converges towards a robust pregrasp policy after 250,000 samples which takes around one hour.

claims.claim: Real-world state estimation was done with a trivially cheap CV pipeline — HSV color segmentation of a red-paper marker plus an OpenCV bounding box from a single RGB camera — rather than learned perception, showing decoupled simple perception can feed a learned manipulation policy.
claims.quote: We first transfer RGB space into HSV space and use an upper and lower bound to segment the side which has already been pasted with a piece of red paper.

claims.claim: Real-world validation used two 7-DoF Franka Emika Panda arms (one as pusher, one as support/gripper) with object masses of 0.08-0.23 kg, so the demonstrated object weights fall inside an SO-101's 0.2-0.3 kg payload class but the platform is far more rigid and precise than a $55 hobby arm, and the method required a second arm or wall as support surface.
claims.quote: The experiments were conducted on the platform Panda Robotic Arms developed by Franka Emika with 7 Degrees of Freedom.

claims.claim: A hybrid gripper combining rigid proximal links with soft passive pneumatic distal joints achieved a 90% success rate grasping sheets of paper when soft-joint pressure was raised to 110 kPa, independent of approach angle.
claims.quote: at a higher pressure of 110 kPa, the success rate of grasping the paper reaches 90%

claims.claim: Finger compliance dramatically relaxes positioning-precision requirements: the hybrid gripper needed only 5 mm of finger travel to reach a 90% grasp success rate, versus about 40 mm for an equivalent rigid gripper — an up-to-eightfold reduction in gripping distance.
claims.quote: the proposed hybrid gripper only needs to move 5 mm to achieve a 90% success rate, while the rigid gripper requires a movement of about 40 mm

claims.claim: The gripper was validated on household objects including a PET bottle, with success rates over 80% for several test objects at 100 kPa input pressure.
claims.quote: over 80% for the toilet paper roll, plastic box, and charger at an input pressure of 100 kPa

claims.claim: Despite the word 'passive' in the title, the design requires active pneumatic infrastructure (an SMC ITV1030 electro-pneumatic regulator and a Nema17 stepper motor), so it is not directly transferable to a $55 SO-101 arm without significant added hardware and cost.
claims.quote: Pneumatic pressure is applied to the cavity

claims.claim: Measured normal grasping forces in comparative paper-grasping tests ranged from roughly 6 to 14 N, an order of magnitude above the sub-Newton fingertip forces typical of hobby-servo grippers, indicating the results were obtained on much stronger hardware than an SO-101.
claims.quote: with normal forces (N₁) ranging from 6–14 N in comparative tests

claims.claim: Grasp points for ungraspable objects can be identified from visual observations alone (using force closure and friction constraints), without force/tactile sensing — relevant to camera-only manipulation on the SO-101 which has no force sensing.
claims.quote: From visual observations, our method first identifies optimal grasp points based on force closure and friction constraints.

claims.claim: Bimanual nonprehensile manipulation achieved a 90% pick success rate over 102 trials in uncluttered scenes and 67% over 45 trials in cluttered scenes in a replica grocery store — establishing that clutter roughly cuts success by a quarter even with a well-engineered two-arm system, a baseline the single SO-101 arm will underperform.
claims.quote: In our replica grocery store, we achieved a 90% success rate over 102 trials in uncluttered scenes, and a 67% success rate over 45 trials in cluttered scenes.

claims.claim: The method targets objects that are bulky, heavy, or irregularly shaped and thus ungraspable by suction or parallel-jaw grippers — a different regime from water bottles and caps, which are conventionally graspable; the paper does not address bottles, caps, low-payload arms, or imitation learning.
claims.quote: many objects in such settings are bulky, heavy, or irregularly shaped, making them ungraspable by conventional end effectors like suction grippers and parallel jaw grippers (PJGs)

claims.claim: When grasp points are occluded, nonprehensile nudging motions can clear obstructions before grasping — a technique (pre-grasp object repositioning by pushing) that could transfer to a hobby arm for singulating piled bottles/caps before picking.
claims.quote: If the grasp points are occluded, a series of nonprehensile nudging motions are performed to clear the obstruction.

claims.claim: A HCMC scrap dealer publicly quotes PET plastic scrap at 16,000-22,000 VND/kg, which directly contradicts the 4,000-7,000 VND/kg PET assumption in the project's existing Vietnam docs; however, this is a dealer marketing page whose headline prices likely represent best-case clean industrial lots, not ve chai street-collected bottles.
claims.quote: Nhựa PET | 16,000 – 22,000đ ... Giá nhựa phế liệu hôm nay【17/07/2026】dao động từ 9.000 đồng/kg đến 39.000 vnđ/kg

claims.claim: PP scrap (the resin of most bottle caps) is quoted at 13,000-30,000 VND/kg, roughly consistent with the research question's premise that cap-grade PP/HDPE fetches ~20-35k VND/kg and is worth separating from bottle bodies.

The dealer's price table has no separate line item for bottle caps (nắp chai) and no distinct HDPE price (PE is grouped generically), suggesting cap-level or resin-level granularity beyond broad categories is not standard in public dealer quotes and cap value must be negotiated as sorted PP/PE.
claims.quote: Nắp chai / nắp nhựa (bottle caps): Not listed on this page ... The page groups PE plastics generically as "Nhựa PE" without specific HDPE pricing.

claims.claim: The dealer states purchase prices depend on plastic type, quality, and market fluctuations, but publishes no explicit premium schedule for sorted vs. mixed, clean vs. contaminated, or minimum quantities — so the marginal VND/kg gain from robotic sorting cannot be derived from this page alone.
claims.quote: Phụ thuộc vào từng loại phế liệu nhựa khác nhau, mà chất lượng, cũng như sự biến đổi của thị trường để định chính xác với giá thu mua

claims.claim: The page's displayed price date is auto-generated (it shows 17/07/2026, one day ahead of the actual current date of 16/07/2026), indicating the 'today's price' framing is a template/SEO artifact rather than a genuinely updated daily market quote, which lowers confidence in the specific numbers.
claims.quote: Giá nhựa phế liệu hôm nay【17/07/2026】dao động từ 9.000 đồng/kg đến 39.000 vnđ/kg

claims.claim: A Vietnamese scrap dealer's current buy-price list pays only 5,000–8,000 VND/kg for PP plastic scrap — the low end of the price table — which undercuts the assumption that PP bottle caps fetch 20,000–35,000 VND/kg at ve chai/dealer level.
claims.quote: Giá phế liệu nhựa PP ... 5.000 – 8.000 [VND/Kg]

claims.claim: The same dealer pays 12,000–19,800 VND/kg for PE plastic scrap (the family that includes HDPE), roughly 2–4x the PP price, meaning separating PE/HDPE items from PP does add real value at the dealer level.
claims.quote: Giá phế liệu nhựa PE ... 12.000 – 19.800 [VND/Kg]

claims.claim: The dealer's price table lists no line item at all for PET bottles, even though the page acknowledges PET as the material of water bottles — consistent with the skeptic finding that PET bottle scrap is low-value/commodity and not separately itemized by this buyer.
claims.quote: Nhựa PET thường được dùng trong sinh hoạt như chai nước, bình đựng nước, vật dụng đựng thức ăn. [No PET price row appears in the price table.]

claims.claim: The dealer identifies ABS as the highest-priced plastic scrap category (13,500–16,000 VND/kg), though its own table lists 'nhựa HI' at 20,500–28,000 VND/kg — an internal inconsistency that flags the page's price data as loose marketing copy rather than audited quotes.
claims.quote: Nhựa ABS có khả năng chịu được nhiệt và lực cao. Đây là loại phế liệu có giá thu mua cao nhất

claims.claim: Scrap prices vary with market supply/demand and material quality, so cleanliness/sorting quality (not just resin type) affects what a load pays — relevant to whether robot-sorted output actually earns the top of a price range.
claims.quote: Tùy theo xu hướng và cung ứng thị trường, chất lượng loại phế liệu khác nhau gây ảnh hưởng đến mức giá thu mua.

result.publishDate: 2026-07-16 (page header states 'cập nhật mới nhất ngày 16/07/2026' — identical to today's date, so it is almost certainly an auto-updating SEO timestamp, not a verified publication date)

claims.claim: A ~GBP 56 (~USD 70) SparkFun Triad Spectroscopy Sensor (AS7265x, 18 channels spanning 410-940 nm visible-to-NIR) can classify six household plastic types (PET, HDPE, PVC, LDPE, PP, PS), making spectroscopy-based plastic ID a plausible cheap sensor add-on for a hobby-budget sorter.
claims.quote: The sole unit cost of the AS7265x sensor is ∼GBP 15, while the integrated triad spectroscopy sensor is ∼GBP 56

claims.claim: Even with the best of ten ML methods (PCA-CNN), the cheap 18-channel sensor only achieves 72.50% mean classification accuracy across the six plastic types — far below commercial NIR sorter performance, so it cannot be relied on as a sole discriminator.
claims.quote: Convolutional Neural Networks and Multi-Layer Perceptron can recognise plastics with a mean accuracy of 72.50% and 70.25%, respectively

claims.claim: PET is the worst-recognized plastic (66% accuracy) with this low-cost sensor, and the largest confusions are exactly the pairs a bottle/cap sorter cares about: PET vs PVC and HDPE vs PP — meaning cheap NIR does not cleanly separate cap plastics (HDPE/PP) from each other.
claims.quote: the smallest accuracy of 66% for PET plastic ... the largest errors are presented for PET being confused with PVC, HDPE with PP

claims.claim: The reported accuracies were obtained under highly controlled lab conditions — samples measured inside a sealed matte-black box at a fixed 50 mm sensor-to-sample distance — so real-world performance on a tabletop arm with ambient light and varying standoff would likely be worse.
claims.quote: a custom-built sealed wood-based box with dimensions of 406 mm × 206 mm × 200 mm...The plastic sample holder is mounted at a 50 mm distance from the sensor

claims.claim: The authors themselves concede the low-cost sensor's accuracy is below that of expensive spectroscopy systems, positioning it as an affordable/portable trade-off rather than a replacement for industrial NIR sorting.
claims.quote: Even though the accuracy achieved is not as high as the accuracy reported by expensive spectroscopy sensors, this work offers a reliable, affordable and portable solution

claims.claim: PET beverage bottles in Vietnam already have a relatively high collection-for-recycling rate (45-65%; PET packaging overall ~50%, the highest of all resins), meaning intact PET bottles are already efficiently captured by existing collection — a hobby sorter adds little value on the bottle-identification step itself.
claims.quote: The CFR rate for PET packaging (including bottles, sheets, and films) is estimated to be a median of 50 percent (based on the mid-point average of the 35 to 65 percent range)... The recycling rate for PET beverage bottles is estimated to be 45 to 65 percent.

claims.claim: Separating caps and sleeves (made of HDPE and PP) from PET bottles is an established, necessary step in Vietnam's PET recycling chain — the study's PET recycling figures are computed only after these other-resin contaminants are removed, confirming cap/bottle separation is a real value-adding operation.
claims.quote: the CFR rate of PET is determined after contaminants like dirt metals and other plastics (sleeves and caps from other materials such as HDPE and PP) have been removed.

claims.claim: Roughly 90% of PET bottles collected for recycling in Vietnam are handled by the informal sector (ve chai pickers, junk shops, aggregators), and HCMC's PET bottle collection rate (62%) is far higher than Hanoi's (39%) — so in HCMC a robot competes directly with an efficient, low-cost informal workforce of ~6,200 collectors.
claims.quote: Previous research by the study team found the CFR rate for PET bottles to be 62 percent in Ho Chi Minh City and 39 percent in Hanoi. According to an estimate, 90 percent of these PET bottles, collected for recycling, are handled by the informal sector... the informal waste collector workforce comprises 6,200 people, i.e., 2,000 street material pickers and 4,200 independent waste pickers.

claims.claim: PP and HDPE have much lower and more uncertain collection rates than PET bottles (each estimated 20-50%, with only easily-separable rigid HDPE at the high end and films/composite applications much lower), indicating the under-collected value pool is in non-PET rigids — which supports focusing a sorter on caps/PP/HDPE rather than PET bottles.
claims.quote: The CFR rate for PP is estimated to be 20 to 50 percent... The CFR rate for HDPE is estimated to be 20 to 50 percent... rate for easily separable rigid HDPE (e.g., for shampoo [bottles is higher]; for other HDPE applications (e.g., films, plastic bags, and so forth), it is estimated to be much lower.

claims.claim: Vietnam recycled only ~33% of key plastic resins in 2019 (1.28 of 3.90 million tonnes of PET/HDPE/LDPE/PP), losing US$2.2-2.9 billion per year in material value, and informal collection is price-elastic — collectors stop collecting when recycled-resin prices fall — so recovered-material prices (not identification technology) are the binding constraint on what separations pay.
claims.quote: As much as 3.90 million tonnes per year (TPY) of PET, LDPE, HDPE, and PP are consumed in Vietnam. Of this, 1.28 million TPY (33 percent) gets recycled... It is common practice for the informal sector to curb operations when buying prices drop for recycled plastics.

claims.claim: A Siamese network with only 4 convolutional layers, trained on RGB images resized to 105x105, achieved ~99.7% accuracy classifying household plastic waste into 5 resin-code categories (PET, PE-HD, PP, PS, Other) on the WaDaBa dataset — suggesting resin-code-level classification from plain RGB is achievable with very lightweight models suitable for cheap hardware.
claims.quote: achieved a high accuracy of 99.74%

claims.claim: The WaDaBa dataset used comprises 4,000 images of household plastic waste across 5 resin categories, collected over four months, with severe class imbalance (PET: 2,200 images vs Other: 40) — indicating a hobby-scale custom dataset (hundreds to low thousands of images) is in the right regime for this task.
claims.quote: Objects...acquired from households over a period of four months.

claims.claim: One-shot/few-shot metric learning (Siamese and triplet-loss networks) is proposed specifically to cope with small plastic-waste datasets, since pair generation from n images yields n^2 training instances — a directly transferable technique for a hobbyist with limited bottle/cap data.
claims.quote: from a data set of size n, a total of n² training instances are generated, which is useful if the size of the data set is small

claims.claim: The 99%+ accuracies are likely optimistic for real deployment: the 80:10:10 split was done per-class on images, with no stated safeguard against the same physical object (photographed at multiple angles/lighting) appearing in both train and test sets, i.e., probable object-level data leakage.
claims.quote: data is split in an 80:10:10 ratio for the training, validation, and test set respectively

claims.claim: Simple color-histogram-based classification of plastics is computationally cheap but the authors judge its accuracy too low for industrial sorting standards, arguing against pure color/heuristic-only pipelines for resin identification.
claims.quote: computationally efficient compared to deep neural networks, the accuracy of classification is low for industrial standards

claims.claim: A PRISMA-guided systematic review identified 112 articles published between 2015 and 2025 on AI-based plastic waste classification and sorting, indicating a substantial and recent evidence base for CV-based plastic-type identification.
claims.quote: This systematic review, following the PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-analyses) guidelines, identified 112 articles published between 2015 and 2025 on AI-based plastic waste classification and sorting.

claims.claim: The current state of the art in plastic waste classification pairs deep-learning vision models (CNNs, YOLO architectures, Transformers) with spectroscopic sensing (NIR, FTIR, Raman) — i.e., pure RGB vision is routinely augmented by spectroscopy in the literature, not used alone.
claims.quote: This study critically reviews the current state-of-the-art classification and sorting methods, such as convolutional neural networks (CNNs), you only look once (YOLO) architectures, and Transformer-based models ... and assesses their integration with advanced spectroscopic techniques such as near-infrared (NIR), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), and Raman spectroscopy.

claims.claim: The review identifies limited real-world datasets, scalability issues, and environmental variability as the key challenges for AI-based plastic classification — directly relevant to deploying a hobby-scale sorter on messy Vietnamese ve chai streams rather than lab benches.
claims.quote: The review highlights the accuracy of identification and classification methods for plastic waste and identifies key challenges, including limited real-world datasets, scalability issues, and environmental variability.

claims.claim: The review's recommended future directions are lightweight models, multi-sensor fusion, and edge-AI deployment — supporting the feasibility premise that low-cost edge hardware (like an SO-101 with cheap cams plus auxiliary sensors) is where the field is heading.
claims.quote: As a novel contribution, this review consolidates performance metrics, maps the diversity of AI-based classification models, and suggests future research directions focusing on lightweight models, multi-sensor fusion, and edge-AI deployment.

claims.claim: Per the article's own caveat (surfaced in search-result text from the ScienceDirect page; not present in the Semantic Scholar abstract copy), reported high classification accuracies come from controlled conditions and restricted datasets, so published accuracy figures should be discounted when scoping a real-world build.
claims.quote: Although high classification accuracies are reported, they are typically achieved under controlled conditions with restricted datasets that may not capture real-world complexities

result.publishDate: 2026-05-01 (issue date; available online ~2026-04-17, per Crossref VOR license start)

claims.claim: A Pi0.5 flow-matching policy fine-tuned via LeRobot on only 100 teleoperated demonstrations achieved an 87-93% success rate (13/15 clean grasp-and-lift, 1 partial) picking a red bottle with an SO-101 Pro arm, with no simulation or domain randomization.
claims.quote: Counting the one grasped-but-incomplete trial as partial successes, the effective success rate is 87–93%. For a first policy trained on 100 episodes with no domain randomization, no simulation pre-training, and a low-cost arm, this is an encouraging baseline.

claims.claim: The reported success rate holds only within a narrow operational envelope (single object, single approximate position, controlled lighting, restricted servo angles) and collapses outside it — the policy was never tested with a second object.
claims.quote: Move the bottle outside that servos envelope and the number drops sharply. Add a second object and the policy has not been tested at all.

claims.claim: The SO-101's STS3215 servos exhibit tracking errors of up to 7 degrees under load on the first three joints, and raising the P gain to reduce this error introduces arm shaking that corrupts the wrist-camera image at the critical pre-grasp moment — a tradeoff the hardware cannot fully resolve via PID tuning.
claims.quote: The first three joints exhibited tracking errors of up to 7 degrees under the current PID values. Increasing the P gain for various D values reduced tracking error but introduced arm shaking — most visibly in the wrist camera during the hold position just before the grasp approach.

claims.claim: A wrist-mounted camera is functionally necessary for reliable grasping with this pipeline: substituting it with dynamically-extracted high-resolution 'foveal crops' from fixed overhead cameras failed to produce reliable grasp behavior because the crops lack a stable egocentric reference frame.
claims.quote: The policy trained on foveal crops failed to develop reliable grasp behavior, and the experiment was abandoned in favor of returning to the physical wrist camera.

claims.claim: Recovery-demonstration data collected by starting episodes from failed arm positions made policy performance worse; the effective method was embedding small lateral corrections within otherwise complete successful trajectories, near the object, and about 30% of the training data included such corrections.
claims.quote: An early attempt at collecting recovery data by starting episodes directly from failed arm positions made performance worse — the arm got stuck in unusual configurations and it had no learned path out of.

claims.claim: ACT on SO-101 needs roughly 50+ teleoperated demonstrations per task for good performance, using a two-camera (wrist + front) setup — matching the user's 2x camera hardware.
claims.quote: 50+ training examples for good performance

claims.claim: A trained ACT policy generalizes poorly beyond the demonstrated object positions, so a bottle/cap sorting task would need demonstrations covering the full range of placements.
claims.quote: Limited generalization to new cube positions

claims.claim: ACT policy performance is brittle to distribution shift: it only performs reliably when deployment conditions (lighting, positions, objects) match the training demonstrations.
claims.quote: Works best when test conditions match training examples

claims.claim: Recommended ACT hyperparameters for SO-101: chunk size 50 (~1.66 s horizon), 15 action steps executed, batch size 4-32, about 7,500 training steps for ~3,000 frames.
claims.quote: Chunk size: 50 steps (~1.66 seconds prediction horizon)

claims.claim: ACT training for SO-101 is feasible on modest hardware, including a Mac CPU or a CUDA GPU, so no expensive compute is required for a hobby-scale sorting project.
claims.quote: Training possible on Mac (CPU) or GPU via CUDA

claims.claim: PET bottle bodies and HDPE caps are already separable by a passive float/sink water bath after grinding (PET flakes sink, HDPE cap flakes float), meaning cap-vs-bottle plastic-type separation does not require vision or spectroscopy at the flake stage — a robotic pre-sort of intact caps competes against this near-zero-cost downstream step.
claims.quote: Caps attached to bottles are made of different plastics (e.g. PET bottle flakes (sinks) vs HDPE caps flakes (float)).

claims.claim: Clear PET commands a higher price than colored or opaque PET in Vietnam, so a color-based (plain RGB camera) clear/colored PET split is a value-adding separation feasible on cheap hardware; rPET grades in Vietnam price at roughly $1,200/ton (high), $1,000/ton (medium), $830/ton (low).
claims.quote: Clear PET has greater value than colored or opaque PET

claims.claim: Vietnam's informal sector (ve chai / waste pickers) collects about half of PET plastic packaging waste nationally, and up to ~90% of PET bottles in major cities, with individual collectors handling roughly 1–6 tons of plastic scrap per month (about 2 tons/month per collector in Ho Chi Minh City) — so any hobby-arm sorter operates inside an already-efficient manual collection chain.
claims.quote: collecting 1–6 tons of plastic scrap and waste per month

claims.claim: Informal-sector rPET sells for $500–$900 per ton versus $800–$1,200 per ton in the formal sector in Vietnam, implying the margin capturable by better sorting/aggregation at the informal level is on the order of a few hundred USD per ton (roughly 7,500–12,500 VND/kg range at collector level).
claims.quote: $500–$900 per ton

claims.claim: In 2024 Vietnam generated 487,714 tons of PET packaging waste with a collection-for-recycling rate of 45%–65% for beverage bottles, 56% (275,559 tons) going unrecycled, ~13% of collected-for-recycling material lost mainly to contamination, and more than 50% of recycled PET sold as fiber to textile industries rather than bottle-to-bottle.
claims.quote: 45%–65% for beverage bottles

claims.claim: An ACT policy trained on an SO-101 with 49 episodes (~39k frames, two camera views) for 100k steps at batch size 1 can complete training with decreasing loss yet produce a robot that stays static or moves unpredictably at deployment — training-loss convergence does not guarantee usable grasping behavior.
claims.quote: robot stays static or moves unpredictably ... loss decreases slowly

claims.claim: Batch size 1 is a primary cause of ACT failure; ACT reportedly requires a batch size of at least 8 for stable gradient estimates.
claims.quote: Batch size 1 is your main problem — ACT needs at least 8 for stable gradients.

claims.claim: Downscaling camera inputs to 480x480 (the resolution used in the original ACT work) incurs no quality loss for tabletop manipulation tasks, implying 640x480 webcams are sufficient for ACT on bottle/cap sorting.
claims.quote: Downscale your cameras to 480×480 (original ACT used this, no quality loss for tabletop tasks).

claims.claim: Datasets dominated by idle/static frames bias an ACT policy toward predicting zero actions, which is a common cause of the robot not moving; trimming static frames from demonstrations mitigates this.
claims.quote: idle/static frames dominating the dataset, biasing the policy toward zero-action predictions.

claims.claim: Even a reasonably-sized ACT run (60 episodes, 200k steps, batch size 32) yields only ~60% success on a fixed-target directional task and ~20% on a moving target, while the same dataset gives 100%/60% on a grasping task — task type strongly affects achievable success rates.
claims.quote: Success rates: 60% (fixed target), 20% (moving target). ... Same dataset achieves 100% and 60% success on grasping task.

claims.claim: ACT trained via imitation learning (100 teleoperated demos per task) on the SO-101 achieved 75% success on simple pick-and-place (Pen Transfer) but 0% success on the selective sorting task requiring object identification among distractors (Selective Color Sorting), directly showing that end-to-end ACT fails at the identify-then-sort structure the bottle/cap task requires.
claims.quote: ACT exhibits extremely high rates of grasp instability, repetition loops, and state mismatch, indicating failures across both perception and action consistency.

claims.claim: Selective sorting (pick the correct object among distractors) was the hardest benchmark task on SO-101 for every evaluated policy, with all methods — ACT, SmolVLA, Wall-X, and pi-0.5 — scoring only 0-10% success, versus 70-95% for plain pick-and-place transfer.
claims.quote: Selective Color Sorting is consistently the most challenging benchmark, with all evaluated policies achieving very low success rates (0-10%). In contrast, Pen Transfer achieves consistently high success rates (70-95%) across all evaluated policies, indicating that simple pick-and-place manipulation is comparatively robust under the current benchmark setting.

claims.claim: On the low-cost SO-101, execution instability (grasp instability and repetition loops) — not semantic misunderstanding — is the dominant failure source across all policies, driven by hardware limits such as actuator noise, joint backlash, and imperfect camera-to-action calibration.
claims.quote: The platform introduces stochasticity into the perception-action loop through actuator noise, joint backlash, control latency, trajectory jitter, limited positional repeatability, and imperfect calibration between visual observations and executed actions.

claims.claim: ACT has very poor failure-recovery ability on SO-101 (6.45% recovery rate vs 30.77% for pi-0.5), meaning an ACT-based sorter will rarely self-correct after a failed grasp — favoring architectures where an external perception/retry loop handles errors rather than relying on the policy.
claims.quote: ACT (6.45%) and SmolVLA (3.23%) exhibit substantially lower recovery rates, indicating limited ability to self-correct once execution failures occur.

claims.claim: The benchmark's training recipe is concrete and replicable on hobby hardware: 100 human teleoperated demonstrations per task collected directly on the physical SO-101 with RGB observations, fine-tuning each policy, and evaluating 20 real-world episodes per model-task pair; the demonstration datasets are publicly released.
claims.quote: For each task, 100 teleoperated demonstrations are collected. ... Each model-task pair is evaluated over 20 independent hardware trials to assess task success, failure behavior, and recovery capability. ... All collected demonstration datasets have been publicly released to support reproducible research.

claims.claim: A robotic waste sorter can sort recyclables using decoupled perception (an SSD object detector trained on autonomously collected images) plus scripted manipulation primitives, rather than end-to-end imitation learning — supporting the 'classifier picks bin, policy/primitive does grasp' architecture for the SO-101 project.
claims.quote: Second, we provide a robotic system that can automatically collect object images to quickly train a deep neural-network model.

claims.claim: The system classifies waste only at the material-category level (aluminum can, glass bottle, plastic bottle) and does NOT distinguish plastic resin types (PET vs HDPE vs PP) from RGB vision — evidence that even academic RGB-based sorters treat 'plastic bottle' as a single class, and resin-level sorting needs priors or other sensing.
claims.quote: the proposed methods enable quick collection of the training image sets for three classes of waste items (i.e., aluminum can, glass bottle, and plastic bottle)

claims.claim: Training data for the waste detector can be collected quickly and cheaply via robot-automated image capture: 59,400 images (120 orientations x 5 viewpoints x 99 objects), with ~12.3 s per 100 images, claimed ~99% faster than prior manual methods — a transferable data-collection strategy for a hobby arm with 640x480 cameras.
claims.quote: 59,400 (120 object-orientation patterns × 5 viewpoint patterns × 99 objects) images ... 12.3 s [for 100 images], representing 99.1% faster than prior methods

claims.claim: Graspless 'push-and-drop' manipulation (pushing the item off the surface into a bin) achieves a faster cycle time (avg 3.3 s) than pick-and-release with a soft five-finger gripper (avg 5.2 s) — directly relevant to a low-payload SO-101 handling crushed/intact bottles, since pushing avoids grasp-force and payload limits entirely.
claims.quote: Push-and-drop: Gripper pushes object in straight line toward bin without grasping; average cycle time of 3.3 s ... Pick-and-release: Soft gripper with five fingers grasps object; average cycle time of 5.2 s

claims.claim: Domain gap between the dataset-collection scene and the deployment scene (illumination, background, scale) materially degrades detector performance, and mitigation via object scaling, histogram matching/equalization, and background synthesis raised mean F-scores to ~79% (aluminum can 72%, glass bottle 89%, plastic bottle 75%) — a caution for training YOLO on home-collected bottle images and deploying in a Vietnamese ve chai setting.
claims.quote: We address differences in illumination and background by applying object scaling, histogram matching with histogram equalization, and background synthesis to the source target-object images.

claims.claim: Peer-reviewed research demonstrates that deep learning on plain RGB images (no NIR/hyperspectral hardware) can classify rigid plastic waste by resin type, and the authors conclude RGB is a practical low-cost alternative to costly spectroscopic identification systems — directly supporting the feasibility of camera-only plastic-type ID on a $55 arm's 640x480 webcams.
claims.quote: The results demonstrate that RGB images offer a practical alternative to other costly and complex systems for effective plastic identification, due to their availability, low cost.

claims.claim: The study's resin classes are ABS, HDPE, PS, and PVC from construction/demolition waste — it does NOT include PET or PP and does not test beverage bottles, so its results transfer only partially to the bottle/cap sorting task (HDPE is covered; the key PET-vs-PP-vs-HDPE cap distinction is not directly validated).
claims.quote: The dataset comprises four commonly used plastic types in construction projects—ABS, HDPE, PS, and PVC.

claims.claim: High classification performance was achieved via transfer learning from ImageNet-pretrained CNN and Vision Transformer architectures (ResNet, ResNeXt, RegNet, Swin Transformer), implying a custom bottle/cap RGB classifier can be fine-tuned from pretrained backbones rather than trained from scratch — a workflow feasible on hobby-scale data and compute.
claims.quote: Advanced Convolutional Neural Network and Vision Transformer-based models, including ResNet, ResNeXt, RegNet, and Swin Transformer, are trained and evaluated on this dataset.

claims.claim: The paper uses knowledge distillation from a large Swin Transformer teacher into lightweight MobileNetV3 student models explicitly targeted at edge-device deployment, indicating resin-type classification can run on cheap/embedded compute of the kind available in a low-budget SO-101 setup.
claims.quote: Knowledge Distillation from a large, computationally intensive, and accurate model to enhance the accuracy of fast and compact models specifically designed for deployment on edge devices, using the output class probabilities of the Swin Transformer model to enhance the accuracy of the lightweight MobileNetV3 models.

claims.claim: The work is motivated by the economics of mixed waste going to landfill because sorting is perceived as financially impractical — a motivation parallel to the ve chai/HCMC context, though the study's setting is Australian construction waste, not municipal bottle streams.
claims.quote: The perceived financial impracticality of sorting and separating waste, coupled with the simplicity of landfilling processes often contribute to mixed material loads sent directly to landfills.

result.publishDate: 2024-10 (available online); journal issue January 2025, Resources, Conservation and Recycling, Vol. 212, article 107937 (DOI: 10.1016/j.resconrec.2024.107937)

result.evidence: Primary source (Sensors 2024, 24(9):2821; open-access via PMC11086069) confirms every specific in the claim. The sensor is the AS7265x SparkFun Triad, an 18-wavelength (410-940nm, vis-NIR) module costing ~£56 — matching "cheap 18-channel sensor." Six plastics were classified (PET, HDPE, PVC, LDPE, PP, PS). Ten ML methods were tested, and PCA-CNN was the best performer at exactly 72.50% mean accuracy (CNN raw 72.17%, PCA-MLP 70.25%, MLP raw 70.08%). Per-type accuracy ranged 66% (PET) to 83.5% (PS). The supporting quote is verbatim and correctly attributed. The claim overreaches on multiple axes. (1) The supporting quote only establishes the sensor's COST (~GBP 56), not classification capability. (2) The paper itself (Sensors, Apr 2024, PMC11086069) reports only 72.5% mean accuracy at best (PCA-CNN), with PET — the single most important class for a Vietnam bottle-sorting application — the WORST class at 66%, systematically confused with PVC; HDPE confused with PP (the exact cap-plastic distinction the research question cares about, HDPE vs PP). "Can classify" at ~2-in-3 correct on PET is not usable sorting performance. (3) Conditions were lab-controlled and non-transferable to an arm-mounted sensor: samples measured at a fixed 50 mm standoff inside a sealed matte-black wooden box with 3D-printed holders fixing distance and angle — ambient light, varying distance, and curved/crushed bottle geometry are all excluded. (4) Dataset weaknesses: only 8 PVC and 15 PS physical items, padded by repeated measurements to 200 spectra per class, creating likely train/test leakage that inflates even the modest reported accuracy. (5) Independent physics constraint: the AS7265x tops out at 940 nm, below the 1100-1700+ nm NIR bands where polymer C-H overtone signatures are strong; black/dark plastics are effectively unmeasurable at Vis-NIR wavelengths (confirmed by black-plastic THz/MIR literature, e.g. Nature Sci Rep s41598-023-49765-z), and thin clear PET is a known weak reflector. result.evidence: The claim overreaches its supporting quote in two ways. (1) The quote is a review's scope statement — it says the review "assesses their integration with" NIR/FTIR/Raman spectroscopy. Assessing integration is not evidence that RGB vision is "routinely augmented by spectroscopy ... not used alone"; a review covering both modalities says nothing about the prevalence of pure-RGB pipelines. (2) The "not used alone" assertion is directly contradicted by a large, active body of RGB-only literature: TrashNet-based classifiers (e.g., RWC-Net, 2,527 RGB images, no spectroscopy), the WaDaBa RGB plastic-waste dataset work (arXiv 2412.20232), and numerous YOLO comparison studies (YOLOv5/v8/v9/v11 reaching ~98% accuracy / 0.99 mAP50 on RGB-only recyclable-waste datasets, e.g. Springer Neural Computing & Applications 2024, ScienceDirect S0950705125000760). RGB-only deep learning is arguably the dominant approach in academic waste-classification papers precisely because spectroscopic hardware is expensive; spectroscopy dominates in industrial MRF sorting (TOMRA-style NIR), while camera-only DL is the norm in the CV literature and in AMP Robotics' commercial system. A defensible weaker claim ("industrial SOTA combines DL vision with NIR; reviews increasingly cover fusion") would survive, but the claim as worded — pure RGB "not used alone" in the literature — is false.

Table 3 confirms exact numbers: Selective Color Sorting — ACT 0%, SmolVLA 5%, Wall-X 0%, pi-0.5 10%; Pen Transfer — 75%/70%/95%/95%. Section 3.1 contains the supporting quote verbatim. Caveats that qualify but do not refute: only 20 trials per model-task pair; non-peer-reviewed preprint; and the sorting task ("put the pink block in the plate and remove the others") also requires removing distractors, so failures mix grounding errors with longer-horizon execution — it is not purely a "pick among distractors" test. The claim faithfully reports the paper's finding and is scoped to this benchmark.
result.counterSource: None found that contradicts the benchmark result itself; note community SO-100/SO-101 demos show ACT succeeding at color sorting in narrower, fixed-layout setups, so the 0-10% figure should not be generalized to all sorting setups.

result.evidence: Primary source verified: arXiv 2606.08881 "Benchmarking Vision-Language-Action Models on SO-101: Failure and Recovery Analysis" (submitted Jun 7, 2026, revised Jun 11, 2026) exists and confirms every factual element verbatim: Table 3 shows ACT at 75% on Pen Transfer and 0% on Selective Color Sorting; "For each task, 100 teleoperated demonstrations are collected" on the physical SO-101; the sorting task's success criterion is "Correctly identifies the target pink object and removes distractors"; and the supporting quote about grasp instability/repetition loops/state mismatch appears verbatim. No contradicting evidence found — community demos (Hackster LeGoRobot, Trelis tutorials) show ACT succeeding only at fixed-target pick-and-place, consistent with the 75%/0% split. Two qualifications that do not rise to refutation: (1) all evaluated policies failed the selective sorting task (SmolVLA 5%, Wall-X 0%, pi-0.5 10%), so the failure is not ACT-specific and may reflect task/demonstration difficulty rather than an ACT-architecture limitation; the paper attributes ACT's failures to "state mismatch" (98% of failed trials) rather than isolating object identification as the cause; (2) it is a single non-peer-reviewed preprint with 20 evaluation episodes per model-task pair. The claim as written (ACT failed the identify-then-sort benchmark) is directly supported; the inferential extension to the bottle/cap task should carry the caveat that VLAs failed too, strengthening the case for decoupled perception over ANY end-to-end policy.

result.evidence: Verified against primary source arXiv:2606.08881 (Yu & Qiu, "Benchmarking Vision-Language-Action Models on SO-101," June 2026): Table 3 confirms ACT scored exactly 75% on Pen Transfer and 0% on Selective Color Sorting, with 100 teleoperated demos per task on SO-101 and 20 real-world eval episodes per model-task pair. The supporting quote about "grasp instability, repetition loops, and state mismatch" appears verbatim. Independent corroboration: LeRobot GitHub issue #545 reports ACT failing when the pick sequence among 6 colored pieces is randomized (only works with fixed spatial order). Two caveats that qualify but do not refute: (1) the paper offers no causal analysis, so "directly showing" failure is due to identify-then-sort structure is an inference (a reasonable one); (2) ALL evaluated policies failed Selective Color Sorting (SmolVLA 5%, Wall-X 0%, pi-0.5 10%), so the failure is not ACT-specific but reflects selective-sorting difficulty at 100-demo scale generally — which strengthens rather than weakens the build-plan implication to decouple perception from grasping.

claims.claim: RGB-image-only classification of plastic waste into resin code categories (PET, PE-HD, PP, PS, Other) is achievable with ~99.7% accuracy using a small Siamese/triplet CNN with KNN, on the WaDaBa benchmark — supporting the feasibility of camera-only plastic-type ID on cheap hardware without NIR spectroscopy.

claims.claim: One-shot/few-shot metric learning (Siamese/triplet networks) makes high accuracy possible with only 4,000 images and no augmentation, because pairwise training generates n-squared instances — relevant to a hobbyist who can only collect a small custom bottle/cap dataset.
claims.quote: these algorithms require a large amount of data for training, often in the range of hundreds of thousands ... [the one-shot technique is suitable] if the size of the data set is small

claims.quote: extreme gradient boosting, partial least squares − discriminant analysis, and convolutional neural network models achieved classification accuracies of 72.50%, 51.21%, and 69.63%, respectively

claims.claim: On real-world black plastics (from waste electrical and electronic equipment), NIR-trained models collapsed to under 10% accuracy — i.e., lab NIR results do not transfer to field waste streams.
claims.quote: external validation on real-world black plastics revealed a sharp performance drop, with accuracies falling below 10% for models trained on NIR-HSI data

claims.quote: With FTIR data featuring characteristic peaks, all three models achieved near-perfect classification accuracy... whereas models trained on FTIR data maintained high accuracy.

claims.claim: The study built a dataset of 49 types of black plastics for training and validating the classifiers, indicating that plastic-type diversity in real waste is much broader than the handful of resin classes a hobby sorter would target.
claims.quote: The study established a high-quality dataset containing 49 types of black plastics to identify plastics types.

claims.claim: Conventional NIR sorting fails on black plastics because carbon black pigment absorbs the NIR signal, so black items (e.g., many dark bottle caps) cannot be identified by polymer type with standard NIR — a key limit if the SO-101 build were to consider an NIR add-on.

claims.quote: These surface layers can influence the measured spectrum by: partially masking the polymer signal, introducing additional spectral features, reducing reflectance intensity.

claims.claim: NIR spectroscopy is the dominant polymer-identification technology in industrial recycling plants (over color cameras, hyperspectral, X-ray), which is the practice used by commercial sorters like TOMRA that does NOT transfer to a $55 RGB-camera arm.

In contrast, Pen Transfer achieves consistently high success rates (70–95%) across all evaluated policies, indicating that simple pick-and-place manipulation is comparatively robust under the current benchmark setting.

claims.claim: ACT, trained via behavior cloning on 100 real-world teleoperated demonstrations per task and evaluated over 20 trials per task on the physical SO-101, achieved 75% on pen transfer, 0% on color sorting, 10% on multi-object packing, 50% on precision placement (33.75% average) — a concrete data-budget and performance baseline for what ACT can do on this arm.

ACT exhibits extremely high rates of grasp instability, repetition loops, and state mismatch, indicating failures across both perception and action consistency.

claims.claim: The SO-101 platform imposes substantially stronger embodiment constraints than research-grade arms — actuator noise, joint backlash, control latency, trajectory jitter, limited positional repeatability, and lower payload — so small execution errors accumulate, especially in long-horizon or contact-rich tasks like handling many bottles/caps in sequence.
