claims.claim: Standard RGB camera images, processed with deep learning, are presented as a viable low-cost alternative to expensive sensing systems (e.g., NIR/hyperspectral) for identifying plastics by resin type — directly supporting the feasibility of resin-type classification with the SO-101's 640x480 RGB cameras.

claims.claim: Accurate resin-type classification can be compressed onto edge-deployable hardware: the authors used knowledge distillation from a large Swin Transformer teacher into a lightweight MobileNetV3 student designed for edge devices, meaning a cheap single-board computer or laptop could run the classifier for a hobby sorting arm.

claims.claim: The study built a specialised dataset of real end-of-life plastic waste covering four resin types (ABS, HDPE, PS, PVC) and trained ImageNet-pretrained CNN and Vision Transformer models (ResNet, ResNeXt, RegNet, Swin Transformer) via transfer learning to classify them with high accuracy — but the class set does NOT include PET or PP, the key resins for the water-bottle/cap task, so transfer to PET-vs-PP-vs-HDPE discrimination is unproven by this paper.

claims.claim: Deep-learning resin classification models were developed as whole-image classifiers on segregated waste fragments (classification by resin type from RGB images), i.e., a decoupled perception approach where a classifier assigns the resin bin — consistent with the 'classifier picks bin, policy does grasp' architecture rather than end-to-end imitation learning.
claims.quote: This paper presents a comprehensive study on RGB images for classification by resin type. A large specialised dataset end-of-life plastic waste gathered.

claims.claim: The economic motivation is that manual sorting is perceived as financially impractical, so mixed plastic loads go to landfill — an Australian construction-and-demolition context whose economics differ substantially from Vietnam's ve chai informal collection system, where manual sorting labor is cheap and already occurs.

claims.claim: A waste-sorting robot distinguished aluminum cans, glass bottles, and plastic (PET) bottles using only RGB-D vision (no spectroscopy or material sensing), reaching a mean F-score of about 79% after augmenting domain-adapted synthetic training data with real-world images (vs ~46% with synthetic data alone). This supports the feasibility of coarse container-category sorting with cheap cameras, but note the system separated container types, not plastic resin types (no PET vs HDPE vs PP discrimination).

claims.claim: The system used a decoupled architecture — a quickly-trainable object detector for perception plus scripted manipulation primitives (not end-to-end imitation learning) — and its training dataset of 59,400 images was collected in about 111 minutes using an automated green-screen setup with a rotating stage, hand-eye robot arm, and AR-marker auto-annotation (about 12.3 s per 100 images).

claims.claim: Measured manipulation performance on real waste items: pick-and-release grasping achieved a 70% mean success rate at ~5.2 s per object, while graspless push-and-drop achieved 57% at ~3.3 s per object; push-and-drop was preferred when feasible because it is ~1.9 s faster. claims.claim: Semi-transparent objects (i.e., clear plastic bottles) were an explicit failure mode of the RGB-D perception pipeline — a semi-transparent object caused misannotation despite depth sensing, and the authors state that future work needs non-optical sensing for such items. This results in the loss of US$2.2 billion to US$2.9 billion per year ... The CFR rate for HDPE is estimated to be 20 to 50 percent. ... The recycling rate for easily separable rigid HDPE (e.g., for shampoo containers) is estimated to be at the higher end while for other HDPE applications (e.g., films, plastic bags, and so forth), it is estimated to be much lower.

claims.quote: Separation of different types of plastic flakes in large water tank Some plastics float and some sink (e.g. caps attached to bottles are made of different plastics (e.g. PET bottle flakes (sinks) vs HDPE caps flakes (float)).

Higher-quality rPET can be sold for about VND25,000–VND30,000/kilogram (kg). ... Clear PET has greater value than colored or opaque PET, which can disrupt the recycling process.

Reported CFRs for PET bottles were 62% in Ho Chi Minh City and in 39% in Ha Noi, with about 90% of these bottles handled by the informal sector.

claims.claim: A Pi0.5 flow-matching policy trained on only 100 teleoperated demonstrations on an SO-101 Pro achieved 87-93% success (13/15 clean grasp-and-lift, 1 partial, 1 positional miss) on a single red-bottle pick task with no simulation or domain randomization.

claims.claim: The SO-101's STS3215 servos exhibit up to 7 degrees of joint tracking error under load, and raising PID gains to fix it induces arm shaking that corrupts the wrist-camera image at exactly the pre-grasp moment — a hardware ceiling on grasp precision independent of the learned policy.

claims.claim: The reported success rate holds only inside a narrow operational envelope (one object, one approximate position range, controlled lighting, restricted servo angles); moving the bottle outside that envelope sharply degrades performance and multi-object behavior is untested.

claims.claim: SmolVLA fine-tuned on the identical 100-episode dataset performed significantly worse than Pi0.5 and failed to produce reliable grasps, suggesting 100 episodes is insufficient for smaller-backbone VLA models to bootstrap manipulation on this hardware.

claims.claim: Recovery demonstrations that start from failure states degraded policy performance; corrections work only when embedded as small lateral adjustments inside otherwise complete successful trajectories, and a wrist camera cannot be substituted by software foveal crops from fixed overhead cameras.

result.evidence: Verified against the open-access full text (PMC11086069, identical to MDPI Sensors 2024, 24(9), 2821; mdpi.com returned 403). The paper tests a ~GBP 56 SparkFun AS7265x Triad multi-spectral sensor (410-940 nm, 18 channels) on six plastics (PET, HDPE, PVC, LDPE, PP, PS). PCA-CNN is confirmed as the best method at exactly 72.50% overall accuracy; PCA-MLP at exactly 70.25%. The supporting quote appears verbatim: "accuracies ranging from a minimum of 66% for PET to the highest accuracy of 83.5% for PS." Authors themselves concede accuracy "is not as high as the accuracy reported by expensive spectroscopy sensors" and call it an initial investigation. Minor imprecision only: the 70.25% figure is PCA-MLP, not plain MLP. The interpretive clause "well below the reliability needed for autonomous value-adding sorting" is a fair reading — 66% per-class accuracy on PET (the key plastic for the use case) is far under the >90-95% purity commercial sorting requires. Peer-reviewed primary source, published April 28, 2024 (current), not marketing. No contradicting evidence found; higher published accuracies use true NIR spectrometers (1000-2500 nm), outside the claim's low-cost-sensor scope.

Verified against the full text via PubMed Central (PMC11086069), which mirrors the paywalled MDPI page (Martinez-Hernandez, West & Assaf, Sensors 2024, 24(9):2821). Every factual element of the claim matches the primary source exactly: (1) PCA+CNN was the best-performing of ten methods with "mean accuracies over all plastic types of 72.5%"; (2) PCA-MLP achieved 70.25%; (3) the six plastic types tested were PET, HDPE, PVC, LDPE, PP, PS; (4) the supporting quote is verbatim from the paper: "accuracies ranging from a minimum of 66% for PET to the highest accuracy of 83.5% for PS"; (5) the sensor is the ~GBP 56 AS7265x module measuring 18 bands from 410-940 nm — notably below the 1000-2500 nm range industrial NIR sorters use, which strengthens rather than weakens the claim's implication. Source quality is appropriate: peer-reviewed journal paper (May 2024), primary source, not marketing. The interpretive tail ("well below the reliability needed for autonomous value-adding sorting") is a fair reading — a 72.5% mean (66% on PET, the highest-volume target) versus the ~95%+ purity commercial optical sorters target means roughly 1 in 4 items misbinned, which would contaminate output streams rather than add value. Counter-evidence search found no credible source disputing the figures; newer low-cost approaches (e.g., 2025-2026 band-selection and data-fusion papers) report improvements but do not contradict this study's result, and the claim is explicitly scoped to this study's sensor. Minor caveat only: the claim should not be generalized to mean all low-cost spectroscopy caps at ~72% — the 410-940 nm AS7265x is not a true NIR (>1000 nm) sensor, and better low-cost results exist — but as written the claim is accurate.

Verified against the open-access full text (PMC11086069, mirror of MDPI Sensors 2024, 24(9):2821, published April 2024): PCA+CNN achieved 72.50% mean accuracy (best of 10 ML methods), PCA+MLP 70.25%, on six plastic types (PET, HDPE, PVC, LDPE, PP, PS) using a ~GBP 75 AS7265x-class 18-channel VIS-NIR sensor; per-class accuracy ranged 66% (PET) to 83.5% (PS), matching the supporting quote exactly. Authors themselves note this is below the 80-99% of expensive specialized sensors, supporting the claim's 'well below reliability needed' framing (commercial sorting requires ~90%+ purity). Attempted refutation found only a 2025 DLSU senior-high-school conference abstract claiming 92.06% (Random Forest) with a similar sensor — non-peer-reviewed, likely controlled/small dataset, insufficient to overturn. Claim is well-scoped to this study's sensor, current, and matched by a peer-reviewed primary source.

result.evidence: The factual core of the claim checks out: ams-OSRAM's own product page confirms the AS7265x is an 18-channel chipset covering 410-940nm (FWHM 20nm), matching the supporting quote. However, the claim's concluding inference — that affordable 'NIR' options for a hobby project "only cover the visible/very-near-IR band, not the 1000-1700 nm region" — is an overreach beyond the quoted source and is directly contradicted by a documented counterexample: the open-source Plastic Scanner project (Jerry de Vos, TU Delft; docs.plasticscanner.com) uses 8 discrete NIR LEDs at 855/940/1050/1200/1300/1450/1550/1650 nm with an InGaAs photodiode and ADS1256 ADC at hobbyist BOM cost (order ~$100-300, far below the GBP 6k floor cited), specifically to identify PET/HDPE/PP/PS/PVC — i.e., an affordable NIR option that DOES sample the 1000-1700nm region commercial sorters use. A 2025 Wiley paper (Werner, Macromol. Mater. Eng., 'Cost-Efficient Detection of Plastics From Post-Consumer Packaging Waste Using Selected Bands in the Near-Infrared Spectrum') further supports low-cost selected-band SWIR sensing. Caveats that keep this from being a total demolition: the Plastic Scanner is discrete-band reflectance (not a full spectrometer), achieved only ~70% classification accuracy, and the project is currently paused — so the practical spirit of the claim (no cheap turnkey SWIR spectrometer exists) survives, but the universal statement as written is false. Also, the MDPI primary source (Sensors 2024, 24(9), 2821) returned HTTP 403, so the GBP 75 / GBP 15 / GBP 6k-50k price figures could not be verified against the paper itself; the quoted sentence only supports the 410-940nm spec, not the pricing or the 'no affordable 1000-1700nm option' generalization.

result.evidence: The factual half of the claim checks out: the AS7265x (ams-OSRAM) genuinely provides 18 channels spanning 410-940 nm, and the source (MDPI Sensors 24(9):2821, 2024, 'Low-Cost Recognition of Plastic Waste Using Deep Learning and a Multi-Spectral Near-Infrared Sensor', PMC11086069) gives roughly the cited costs (~GBP 15 chipset, ~GBP 56-75 for the SparkFun Triad module, which retails at $69.95). However, the claim's load-bearing conclusion — that affordable options for a hobby project 'only cover the visible/very-near-IR band, not the 1000-1700 nm region' — is contradicted by a well-documented counterexample: the open-source Plastic Scanner (Jerry de Vos, docs.plasticscanner.com; Hackaday 2021), which uses 8 discrete NIR LEDs at 855/940/1050/1200/1300/1450/1550/1650 nm plus a single InGaAs photodiode (900-1700 nm responsivity) with an ADS1256 ADC, is hobbyist-buildable for on the order of USD 100-300 (not GBP 6k-50k), and was built specifically to discriminate PET, HDPE, PVC, LDPE, PP, and PS. Peer-reviewed work (Werner 2025, Macromolecular Materials and Engineering, 'Cost-Efficient Detection of Plastics From Post-Consumer Packaging Waste Using Selected Bands in the Near-Infrared Spectrum') independently confirms that selected-band NIR in the 1000-1700 nm region is a viable low-cost approach. The claim conflates 'cheap full spectrometers do not exist' (true) with 'cheap sensing in the 1000-1700 nm band does not exist' (false — discrete-LED + InGaAs photodiode designs cover exactly the bands commercial sorters exploit, minus black-plastic limits which apply to commercial NIR too). For the build plan this distinction matters: the claim would wrongly steer the project away from a ~$150-300 discrete-NIR add-on that is the single most direct route to PET/HDPE/PP discrimination.

result.evidence: Primary source verified via PMC (open-access mirror of the MDPI paper, PMC11086069): the paper states verbatim that the AS7265x measures "18 wavelengths" from "410 nm (ultraviolet—UV) to 940 nm (infrared—IR) with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) bandwidth of 20 nm", that "the sole unit cost of the AS7265x sensor is ∼GBP 15", and that traditional spectroscopy sensors are "highly expensive (GBP 6k–50k)". One minor inaccuracy: the paper prices the integrated triad module at ~GBP 56, not ~GBP 75 (GBP 75 likely conflates the SparkFun Triad's ~$75 USD retail price) — same order of magnitude, doesn't alter the conclusion. The 1000–1700 nm inference is not in the paper but is physically sound (silicon detectors cut off ~1000-1100 nm; commercial plastic sorters use InGaAs SWIR per Sensors Unlimited), and the paper's own mediocre results in the 410-940 nm band (72.5% mean accuracy, 66% on PET across 6 plastic types) corroborate that this band is marginal for resin identification. Closest counterexample — TI DLP NIRscan Nano EVM (900-1700 nm, ~$1,000) — covers the SWIR region but at ~18x the $55 arm's cost, so the 'affordable for a $55-arm project' framing holds.

result.evidence: Primary source verified via PMC full text (PMC11086069, Sensors 2024, 24(9):2821). The paper's best configuration (PCA-CNN, 72.5% mean accuracy) reports per-class confusion-matrix accuracies "ranging from a minimum of 66% for PET to the highest accuracy of 83.5% for PS" — PET was the single worst-recognized class in that configuration and also the worst (60%) in the raw-CNN configuration. The sensor is the ~£56 AS7265x (410-940 nm) multi-spectral module, matching the "cheap-sensor approach" framing, and 66% accuracy fairly supports "misclassified roughly one time in three." Peer-reviewed 2024 primary source, current, not marketing. Caveats not rising to refutation: samples were cut/flattened ~50mm plastic pieces (not intact bottles), and one weaker sub-configuration (LDA-CNN) had PVC score lower than PET; the claim is correctly tied to the 66% PCA-CNN result.

Claim survives adversarial checks. (1) Source verified as genuine and high-quality: "AI-based plastic waste classification for sorting purposes: A review on recent progresses and challenges" is a PRISMA-guided systematic review in Waste Management (Elsevier, 2026) covering exactly 112 articles from 2015-2025 — matching the claim's stated literature window. result.evidence: The 99.72%/99.79% figures are genuinely reported in arXiv 2011.07747, but the claim overreaches from them. (1) Dataset leakage: WaDaBa (Bobulski & Piatkowski 2018) was built from only ~100 physical objects photographed ~40 times each under different rotations/lighting to yield 4,000 images. The paper uses an 80:10:10 image-level split, not an object-level split, so the same physical bottle appears in both train and test at different angles. The ~99.7% therefore measures re-recognition of already-seen objects, not classification of novel bottles — the relevant task for a sorting arm. The paper itself concedes that augmentation-heavy approaches "do not work well on new instances of data that do not have a rotational variant in the training set." (2) Class imbalance: PET is 2,200 of 4,000 images while 'Other' has only 40, so average accuracy is dominated by the easiest, most frequent class. (3) Contradicting domain evidence: NIR/hyperspectral sorting literature (Qualitas Technologies, Solid Scanner, Konica Minolta, and peer-reviewed NIR studies) states that PET, HDPE, PP, PS are frequently visually indistinguishable — overlapping colors, textures, transparency — and that resin-level discrimination depends on molecular absorption features in the 1000–2500nm band, outside RGB's 380–700nm range. RGB can exploit shape/color/product priors (useful heuristics for bottles vs caps), but cannot generally resolve polymer chemistry. (4) Source quality: a 2020 non-peer-reviewed arXiv preprint reporting a near-ceiling number on a tiny lab benchmark is insufficient to support the strong feasibility claim 'camera-only plastic-type ID without NIR.' The narrow benchmark statement is true; the feasibility inference attached to it is not supported and is contradicted by the physics of polymer identification.

result.evidence: The quoted numbers (Siamese KNN 99.72%, Triplet KNN 99.79%) are real, but the claim's inference — that this supports camera-only resin-type ID on cheap hardware — collapses under a train/test leakage problem. WaDaBa's own construction (Bobulski & Piatkowski 2017, wadaba.pcz.pl) is ~100 distinct physical objects photographed ~40 times each under different rotations/lighting to make 4,000 images (PET 2200, PE-HD 600, PP 640, PS 520, Other only 40 ≈ 1 object). The arXiv 2011.07747 paper splits 80:10:10 BY IMAGE, not by object, so virtually every test image has ~30+ other photos of the SAME physical bottle in the training set. A KNN over Siamese/triplet embeddings in that regime is doing instance re-identification (matching a test photo to other views of the same object), not resin classification — the 99.7% figure measures memorization of ~100 objects, not the ability to infer polymer chemistry from RGB on unseen items. The paper acknowledges no such limitation and never tests on held-out objects or in-the-wild images (single objects, controlled backgrounds, lab lighting). Source quality is also mismatched to claim strength: a 2020 arXiv preprint (DICTA workshop extension) with no per-object split and no external validation cannot carry an extraordinary claim that contradicts industry practice — MRF sorters (TOMRA, AMP) use NIR precisely because resin type is a chemical property RGB cannot observe: an HDPE cap and a PP cap of the same color are visually indistinguishable, and clear bottles can be PET or PP with identical appearance. RGB CV can exploit shape/color/context priors (e.g., 'clear beverage bottle → almost certainly PET in Vietnam'), which is a heuristic-prior argument, not what this benchmark demonstrates. The claim as stated is an overreach from a leaky, saturated lab benchmark.

(1) Quote-to-claim fit: the source quote ("Due to their low reflectance in near-infrared (NIR) spectroscopy, caused by carbon black absorption, black plastics were traditionally considered unidentifiable") directly supports the claim's mechanism (carbon black absorbs NIR → low reflectance → featureless spectra); the claim actually softens "unidentifiable" to "hard to identify," which is appropriately calibrated. (4) Source quality: peer-reviewed 2025 Measurement (Elsevier) article, consistent with the broader literature; not marketing. (5) Not outdated: the source is from 2025 and the physics is unchanged. result.evidence: Overreach. The source (Resources, Conservation & Recycling, Oct 2024, S0921344924005305) is a real peer-reviewed paper and the quoted methodology is accurate: knowledge distillation from a Swin Transformer teacher into MobileNetV3 students. But the claim's payload — "meaning a cheap single-board computer or laptop could run the classifier for a hobby sorting arm" — is not supported for this research question, on three grounds. (1) Wrong resin classes: the paper's dataset comprises four construction-and-demolition plastics — ABS, HDPE, PS, and PVC — it contains NO PET and NO PP, the two resins central to the bottle/cap sorting task (PET bottles, PP/HDPE caps). "The classifier" from this paper cannot sort bottles and caps; only the training recipe transfers, and it would require building a full custom PET/HDPE/PP dataset from scratch. (2) Edge deployment is design intent, not demonstrated: the abstract says the compact models are "specifically designed for deployment on edge devices" — no evidence surfaced (abstract, ADS record, secondary citations) that the authors actually deployed on an SBC or reported inference latency on edge hardware; the full text is paywalled (403). The uncontroversial part — MobileNetV3 runs on a Raspberry Pi/laptop — is generic prior knowledge, not a finding of this paper. (3) Generalization of "accurate": accuracy was measured on a curated, single-domain Australian C&D dataset of rigid plastics; it does not establish that RGB-only resin identification stays accurate for visually ambiguous cases (clear PET vs clear PP, crushed/labeled bottles) that dominate the bottle-sorting task. The narrow claim "KD compresses a Swin teacher into an edge-oriented MobileNetV3 with enhanced accuracy on their 4-class C&D dataset" would pass; the claim as written, positioned as evidence the hobby arm's resin classifier problem is solved on cheap hardware, is an unsupported extrapolation.
